speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. Today is November 15, 1996. I'm interviewing Margaret Lowe. And my name is Judith Ehrenfeld. All right, Margaret, you were telling me when the tape ended that you were living with a friend when your parents moved. And you wanted to tell me something about why.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my parents were moved into that other apartment there was just no room for a third person. And I was shortly before leaving for England, so, so my friend's parents that lived nearby where we used to live took-- you know, took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish family. And she also left as-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH], and she also left with the kindertransport before me. And she left. And she was taken in by two English maiden ladies, teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when I left on the kindertransport on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] April 18, 1939, and-- Could you tell me about those arrangements? How did you get connected with the kindertransport? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, as I said, a cousin of my mother's was a teacher at a Jewish school in Berlin. And I think she had heard about the kindertransport. And she put my parents in touch, I guess, with the organization. I don't know. And it was no need for me. I only had to travel with them because I was being taken in by these friends of my parents. So I wasn't under the auspices of the committee that, you know, arranged it. I just needed permission to travel with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that permission?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess my parents arranged it. And I don't remember anything about it. I wasn't actively involved. I only know that then it started making-- having clothes made for me and buying this and that and equipping me. And then they came. You couldn't just pack your stuff. They sent inspectors that supervised that you don't take anything out that you shouldn't. And you know, there were all kinds of things that were forbidden-- certain books that had to not have in the house or whatever. And you couldn't take out silver or jewelry or whatever. So they had somebody that came to your house and actually did the packing for you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hide anything to take out with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean, as I said before, we weren't into that sort of thing. I mean, you wouldn't have taken the risk. And you were allowed-- you were allowed two silver knives, forks, and spoons per person. So I had just that and, and you know, my clothing and so on. And, and then I left on that day for the kindertransport. And my parents saw me off, and these Chinese friends that I had mentioned before. And I was very sorry-- you know, a lot of people were happy to leave. But I was sort of very sorry to leave. And at other extent, I expected to see my parents soon again. So that wasn't so traumatic because they were going to leave very soon after for Switzerland. They had only three months permission to stay in Switzerland. And then they were going to go to England. And what I forgot to say is that after the Kristallnacht, or maybe just before, my parents first went to get a number for America, because you needed a number for immigration. And of course, by that time, their number was so sky high that it would take years before the number came up. So they were planning to spend time in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- I didn't, you know, mind leaving my parents because I didn't think it was going to be for very long. And-- That was in April of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16 and 1/2. And then I left-- then I was all night on the train. And just recently at one of the kindertransport reunions, somebody came and said she knew me. And I didn't remember her. But we talked about it. And she right away knew my maiden name was or whatever. And we talked about it. And we established that we were at that kinderheim, you know, where the-- together and that we were on the same kindertransport. So I was very moved because I don't know anybody who knew me back then. And it's very hard that you have never anybody to compare memories with. Anyway, I was on that kindertransport. And it took eight-- we left at 12 midnight--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Munich, from the main station. And 8 o'clock next morning, we were in Frankfurt. And there my uncle and aunt, my mother's sister, met me. And they brought me some goodies. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you expect them to meet you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I knew. And the train went on. And then we came to Dusseldorf. And by that time I had been on the train for 10 hours. My future husband-- other children came. And amongst others-- I mean, at the time I didn't know. We didn't talk or anything. But he was in another compartment. And he was on the same train. And then we crossed into, into Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having to cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the border, at the border, they bought us cocoa-- well, they came in, the Germans, I guess, came in and looked through the luggage. And I have since heard that some people were worried about it. I don't remember being very concerned. But-- so-- when we crossed the border at the first Dutch stop in Holland, they gave us cocoa. But I wasn't very impressed because the cocoa was made with water. And we were-- I was used to-- but anyways, they gave these poor refugee children, they poured us cocoa. And then we came to, I guess, Rotterdam. And there my other uncle, my father's brother, met me. And he brought me a huge package with all this, you know, chocolate and cookies and food and whatever. And in the excitement of that, I lost my camel hair blanket that I was carrying with my luggage. But only recently, my cousin who lives now in America, in New York, told me-- I had no memory of it-- that they were hidden or living-- not hidden, but living illegally in Holland at the time. And, and she came with her father evidently to meet me at the train. And she was so shocked. She thought that was such a sad sight seeing all the children with their little name tags hanging from their neck. I mean, I didn't feel so bad. But she said that was to her-- she's two year older than I-- a terrible sight. She was telling me so just recently. Anyway, then we embarked, and we were on a ship. And altogether, it took us 36 hour to reach England. And at night, as I said, we were overnight on the ship. And then we got to Harwich. And there we went into a train. And we were given again a box of-- a box lunch. And eventually, we came to London. And I was picked up by Mr. Gunther. And he took me home on the Underground. And I became part of their family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their family like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their family was they were an elderly couple. They had no children. They were upper class English people and very good to me. But it was very difficult for me. It's-- you know, it was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what was difficult about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't know what-- you know, what a young girl-- they couldn't relate to that. And they were-- you know, this wasn't proper and that wasn't proper. And I really didn't have any friends. They were good to me when at that time I did fast on Yom Kippur. So she would keep food for me and gave me the dinner at night. And you know, she didn't interfere one way or another if I went to synagogue or whatever I did. And eventually, I became-- I went to the committee to get apprenticed to something. And at that time I still had communication with my parents. First, direct, then after the war broke out through my uncle in Holland and later with my other relatives in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got there, you were 16 and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were expecting your parents to be there in a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, I still had letter communication--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I asked them about every day what I should do then or there. And then when it came that I should have to choose a-- choose a profession, the only choices I had was millinery, hair dressing or dressmaking. And none of those three appealed to me. Not that I knew what I did want to do, but I knew I didn't want to. And my parents sort of made the decision. And well, first, I knew I didn't like sewing. Anyway, millinery was the chosen thing. And I was apprenticed to a milliner at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family were living with made those arrangements for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, he took me when I had to go to the committee, he took me along to the-- you know, to the-- there on the subway and so on. But I think in the end, I saw something in the paper, and I applied for it myself. And I was apprenticed as a milliner. And I remember the day the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When Poland was invaded, on the 1st of September, I was an apprentice in that millinery shop. And I started-- I cried really hysterically because I thought that now my parents had no more chance to get out. And they-- it was a big place, almost like a factory. And they brought me into the head person. And they have very, very supportive and nice. But really, they didn't understand it. They didn't understand it. And tried to calm me down. And I said, now my parents will be killed and so on. And they still did not believe these things to be happening. And, and but it happened to have come to pass. So I didn't-- you know, hope springs eternal. Right till the end of the war I still had hope, but against my better judgment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war broke out, what kind of communication did you have with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for a while yet through Switzerland and first Holland because my uncle was still living in Holland until 1941 when he was able to reach America. And he tried to get my parents out. He did try, you know, various things, through Cuba and-- but as I said, my parents never-- my parents always wrote they are still waiting. The furniture was sent out, and they were still waiting for the passports. The passports were confiscated at Kristallnacht. And my parents never applied. They applied and never got it. And there were always further postponements. And on the day after war broke out, their passport-- now the boundaries, the frontiers were closed. And the visas were not anymore in effect. And I always believed that this was a sort of revenge that they-- that my father had at that time fought them over the apartment and not in uncertain terms. And they compromised about it. And I think later got their revenge. I mean, I have nothing to prove it. But that's, that's my theory. And then my parents, I only later found out, that they were departed to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, the Red Cross, I think, made inquiries. And I got it through a letter from the Jewish Gemeind-- the Jewish authority in Munich sent me a copy of the report.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they were deported wen?

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to Riga. I don't know the date, the date of that transport. But I know the date of death was determined as 31st of December 1940-- '41, I believe, yeah. I had recently found a letter where-- that had been written to my parents where they were already expected, I guess, to be deported. And it was sort of the last letter from some other relative. And how that letter came into my hands, I don't really know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the last letter that you got from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? I don't know. And I was thinking the other day when I looked through all my papers that I had all those letters. And I haven't looked at them in years. And now I suddenly couldn't find them. I never, never read, read or look. But I know I have some. And when I was looking, usually my husband if anything had to be looked up, he looked it up. I didn't want to. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was-- you know, after the war broke out, we were interviewed. In England, they had a tribunal. And some people were interned. And if you were a friendly alien you were not interned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, of course, I still had communication with my parents. And when that man who interviewed me was very-- was very-- oh, what shall I say-- considerate, you see. And he said, you don't hear anything from your patents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember being very aware not to get caught in anything. And I said no. And he was so sympathetic and so on. But I really still had letters, but it wasn't quite legal. It was via Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were declared a friendly alien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was not interned. But this Mr. Gunther was interned because he was German. His father was a German officer in the previous war, I guess. And he was interned. And I lived with Mrs. Gunther at that time. And at that time she asked me to call her Auntie Hilde. And-- he came-- his mother was English. In fact, she was English nobility. But the father was a German, so he was considered an alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was interned through the whole war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was interned for maybe a month or so. I'm not sure exactly. He was an old man at that time already and certainly not a-- no threat. But anyway legally I guess he was a German national, or at least of German background.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- after you became an apprentice for the milliner and then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So then I worked there for a while. And we got bombed. When can I tell about my husband? I really want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you tell me about meeting him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When, when we were bombed, and I had a cousin living in Birmingham. And he was much older. And he was a Swiss cousin. And so he was an established business man in Birmingham. And my father had written to him to look out for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we were bombed in London, he wrote that if-- oh, he invited me for Christmas to spend the holiday with him. And then later on, he said, if the bombing get too bad, you come to Birmingham. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now, of course, he wasn't married. And he didn't know what to do with a 16-year-old girl. So he put me up with one of his girlfriends. And she was a very sweet woman. And I lived there for a little while, till they found me a place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I lived with an English-- I put an ad in the paper. We put an ad in the paper. And I lived with an English woman whose husband was in the service. And she wanted somebody in the house with her, so. I, actually, when we looked there, I right away didn't like her. And I was right, and I chose another place. And this was a beautiful house, and my cousin thought I should live there. And I liked another family better.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he agreed. I went to this other family. And they were-- seemed like lovely English people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the night that my cousin moved me in there was a terrible air raid. And we had to go and report to the police. I had to report, you know, my new address to the police. And my cousin and I were caught in this terrible air raid. And this was in a place where there was embankment of guns. And everything shook. And we had to spend the night at the police station. And meanwhile these people were worried we didn't come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, my cousin said, you're not going to stay there. This other place, this other woman was a little more further out in the suburbs. And you're not going to stay where it's so dangerous. And so I moved with this woman who-- where I stayed for several years or so. She was not a very nice person. My first-- my first instincts were right. How was she not nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really say. She was just not a nice person. She stole some of my stuff That was one of the not nice things. But eventually, I made friends with a group of German Jewish refugees. And then I moved into another area of Birmingham where more of us lived and just took a room with like in a rooming house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was years you lived with this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived-- I am not sure now if it's two years or four years. I did live quite a number of years with-- no, it must have been two years only. And then I moved and lived two years. And because-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do the two years that you live with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked again in the millinery. And then eventually I had to do war work. And then I worked in a lab that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was war work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, war work was a factory or something else. So this at that time was a metallurgical lab. They analyzed the ingot, aluminum, ingot for making planes and so on. And-- But when I was in this German Jewish group-- it wasn't just Jews. There were also just Germans, young Germans that were refuges. And that's where I met my husband. And he came there too. And we met. And that's when we established that we had been on the same kindertransport and arrived on the same day in Germany-- in England. And he had boarded the same train in Dusseldorf. And he had come to also a non-Jewish people in, in Cambridge, a professor, Professor Houghton and Mrs. Houghton. And his brother was-- these people were very active. And his brother was with those people, too. And my husband had an aunt living also in Cambridge. And she was a dentist there. And he used to go there to get more food because these very fancy English people had very small appetites and were not geared to feeding a growing young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was apprenticed already in Germany as a plumber. And so they sent him-- this woman, this Mrs. Houghton was very active in refugee work. Also, as I said, they were not Jewish, but they were very politically conscious. And she took a whole lot of children in and sort of recycled them. You know, she moved them on, and then the next ones took their place. So my husband was sent to Northampton to be apprenticed to a plumber. And he lived in a terrible house, which was-- he wrote it was dirty. But it was probably more immoral than dirty. And he knew what was going on but, you know, wasn't used to such conditions. So eventually the committee moved him away from there. Were you writing to him at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time, I hadn't met him yet. Then when he later on came to Birmingham-- I don't-- I think he came to Birmingham because he had an uncle living at Birmingham. And he eventually got to Birmingham and worked there. And then I met him in this youth group. And then when he was 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was interned because men were all interned. But he was interned on the Isle of Man. And then after due time, I guess he got released and worked also in the plumbing field. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, you were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Birmingham.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the group with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: With that-- yes, that's when I-- when-- that's when I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he told me that he was volunteering for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English army, the British army. But early on, they couldn't volunteer. They could only go in what was called the Pioneer Corps. And so he waited till you could join the proper English army. And he was in the Royal Engineers and served for, I guess, four years in the British army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were writing to him during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing those four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was working-- well, I worked in that lab. And at that time, it was terrible, terrible conditions. And so one fine day, I went to the Labor Department. And I said, I'm not going back there. Probably had almost a nervous break-- it was just terrible. And they closed the place down because eventually they had other complaints. And then I worked in a factory in, in Birmingham.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- let me see. I worked-- yes, probably by '42, I don't quite know the chronology, but about-- I lived in Birmingham till about '45. I guess I was still in Birmingham when the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, It was very exciting. Of course, it was very exciting. Everybody danced in the streets. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you here, Margaret.
